title: Tweak on super has ‘no defence’
full_text:
The SMSF Association has hit out at Labor’s move to ban self-managed funds from borrowing to invest in property, saying the change will reduce investment flexibility and does not address the underlying problem flagged by the Greens.
“We’re disappointed the government has given in to the Greens demands,” SMSF Association chief executive Peter Burgess said, warning Labor’s tinkering with super would erode confidence in the system.
“There’s been review after review undertaken on these borrowing rules and they have found no systemic risks to the housing market from these arrangements,” he said.
“If the concern is property spruiking and high-pressure sales tactics, which is what the Greens have been saying they’re concerned about, then they should be addressing that behaviour and not making changes to the investment rules which will reduce investment flexibility.” Mr Burgess said the SMSF Association was not consulted on the rule change, which was announced by Prime Minister Anthony Albanese on Tuesday, allowing the government to get its controversial federal budget changes over the line. “Whenever there are changes to super, it erodes confidence in the system,” he said. “And confidence is important, especially when we’re asking people to make voluntary contributions to super. We’re talking here about rules that have been settled for two decades and now they’re changing them just to secure passage of their budget measures.”
